title: High fever, rash, and lightheadedness

chart.cc: Fever, diffuse rash, and feeling faint
chart.oneLiner: 23F presents with about a day of high fever, a diffuse sunburn-like rash, vomiting/diarrhea, myalgias, and lightheadedness, now hypotensive.

opening: I spiked a high fever last night, my whole body looks like a sunburn, and I keep feeling like I'm going to pass out.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 23-year-old woman, ill-appearing, flushed, and lightheaded. Cooperative but feels awful and is a little dazed.

HPI: About 24 hours of abrupt high fever and chills, diffuse muscle aches, headache, sore throat, vomiting and watery diarrhea, and worsening lightheadedness/near-syncope when she stands. Noticed a red 'sunburn' rash spreading over her trunk and limbs today. She has been menstruating and has been using high-absorbency tampons, leaving the last one in much longer than usual (only if asked about menstruation/tampon use/recent gynecologic history). NO recent travel, NO tick exposure, NO sick contacts, NO new medications/antibiotics (argues against a drug reaction), NO neck stiffness or photophobia (only if asked). She has not yet noticed any peeling of the skin (desquamation typically comes 1–2 weeks later).
PMH: healthy, no chronic illness. MEDS: combined oral contraceptive only; NO recent new drugs. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol, nonsmoker, in a monogamous relationship. GYN: regular menses, currently menstruating, uses tampons.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, flushed, diaphoretic, mildly confused/dazed but able to answer.
- Vitals: BP 82/48, HR 126, RR 24, 39.6°C, SpO2 96% RA; appears hypotensive with a wide pulse and warm extremities (distributive/warm shock).
- Skin: diffuse blanching macular erythroderma resembling a sunburn over trunk and extremities, including palms and soles; NO petechiae, NO purpura, NO vesicles/bullae, NO target lesions, NO Nikolsky sign; no desquamation yet.
- HEENT: conjunctival injection (red eyes) without discharge; oropharyngeal hyperemia ('strawberry'-appearing red tongue/throat); mucous membranes dry.
- Neuro: drowsy/mildly confused but no focal deficit; neck supple, NO meningismus.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur; warm, well-perfused extremities with brisk capillary refill despite hypotension.
- Lungs: clear. Abdomen: soft, mildly diffusely tender, no peritoneal signs.
- Pelvic (if performed): retained tampon in the vaginal vault with surrounding erythema/discharge; no abscess.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the tampon use / retained tampon unless asked about menstruation, tampon or sanitary-product use, recent gynecologic history, or a pelvic exam is performed.
- The rash distribution (palms/soles), conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, and warm-shock perfusion are revealed only if specifically examined.
- Desquamation has NOT happened yet; if asked about skin peeling, say it has not peeled.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 18.5 with marked left shift (bandemia); Hgb normal; Plt 95 (mild thrombocytopenia).
labs.BMP_Renal: Cr 2.1 (acute kidney injury, baseline normal); BUN elevated; mild metabolic acidosis — supports multi-organ involvement.
labs.LFTs_CK: AST/ALT and total bilirubin elevated (>2x normal); CK elevated from myositis — multi-system involvement (a TSS criterion).
labs.Lactate_Coags: Lactate elevated (~3.5) consistent with shock; INR/PTT mildly prolonged, fibrinogen preserved (early; watch for DIC).
labs.Cultures_Workup: Blood cultures drawn (often NEGATIVE in staphylococcal TSS — toxin-mediated); vaginal/wound and throat cultures sent; urine HCG NEGATIVE; menstrual history positive for tampon use with a retained tampon found on pelvic exam.
labs.Serologies: Acute hepatitis panel, monospot, and Rocky Mountain spotted fever/leptospirosis serologies pending/negative — help exclude mimics.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia (~125) with no acute ischemic changes; nonspecific findings consistent with fever and distributive shock.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia (~125) with no acute ischemic changes; nonspecific findings consistent with fever and distributive shock. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lungs without consolidation or effusion; an early study may be normal (watch for ARDS/pulmonary edema if shock progresses).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Clear lungs without consolidation or effusion; an early study may be normal (watch for ARDS/pulmonary edema if shock progresses). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: normal PA chest radiograph with clear lungs

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this febrile young woman with a diffuse rash and hypotension (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Toxic shock syndrome (leading,7). Septic shock from another source / meningococcemia (CANT-MISS to exclude,5). Necrotizing soft-tissue infection / occult source (CANT-MISS,3). Rocky Mountain spotted fever or other tick-borne/severe rash-illness (core,2). Drug reaction (DRESS/SJS-TEN) (core,3). Other viral exanthem or scarlet fever (core,2). Names a toxin-mediated distributive shock and the imperative to find and remove the source (e.g., retained tampon) (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Toxic shock syndrome. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Septic shock / meningococcemia; Necrotizing soft-tissue infection or other occult source. Also reasonable: Rocky Mountain spotted fever / tick-borne illness; Severe drug reaction (DRESS/SJS-TEN); Other viral exanthem or scarlet fever.

criticalActions.item: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection or occult source

coreActions.item: Rocky Mountain spotted fever / tick-borne illness
Other viral exanthem or scarlet fever
Names toxin-mediated distributive shock and the imperative to find and remove the source (e.g., retained tampon)

mustNotMiss: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection or occult source
acceptableAlternatives: Rocky Mountain spotted fever / tick-borne illness
Other viral exanthem or scarlet fever

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC with differential and platelets (2). Comprehensive metabolic panel / renal and hepatic function (CANT-MISS — document multi-organ involvement,3). CK for myositis (2). Lactate and coagulation studies (CANT-MISS,3). Blood, vaginal/wound, and throat cultures plus urine HCG and source identification, including pelvic exam for a retained tampon (CANT-MISS,3). Serologies to exclude mimics (RMSF, hepatitis, monospot) (2). PENALTY: failing to look for and remove the source / not performing a pelvic exam in a menstruating woman (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC with differential; comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and hepatic function); CK; lactate and coagulation studies; blood cultures plus vaginal/wound and throat cultures and urine HCG; pelvic exam to find and remove a retained tampon; serologies to exclude mimics.

criticalActions.item: Comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and hepatic function)
Cultures plus source identification and pelvic exam for retained tampon

penalties.item: Failing to identify/remove the source or not performing a pelvic exam in a menstruating woman

requiredTests: Comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and hepatic function)
Cultures plus source identification and pelvic exam for retained tampon
contraindicatedTests: Failing to identify/remove the source or not performing a pelvic exam in a menstruating woman
orderSensitiveActions: Failing to identify/remove the source or not performing a pelvic exam in a menstruating woman

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes compensatory sinus tachycardia from fever/shock with no acute ischemic changes (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia or missing the rate (-3).
keyFindings: systematic read confirming no acute ischemic change
commonMistakes: failing to attribute the tachycardia to fever/distributive shock
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia (~125) without acute ischemic changes; a compensatory response to fever and distributive shock.

coreActions: systematic read confirming no acute ischemic change

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a clear/normal film (6); notes it screens for a pulmonary source and a baseline to watch for ARDS as shock evolves (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies clear lungs / no acute process
notes value as a baseline and to exclude a pulmonary source
commonMistakes: inventing consolidation or effusion on a normal film
failing to use it as a baseline for evolving ARDS
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lungs with no consolidation or effusion; a normal baseline that also helps exclude a pulmonary source and to monitor for ARDS.

coreActions: identifies clear lungs / no acute process
notes value as a baseline and to exclude a pulmonary source

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: High fever, hypotension, diffuse macular erythroderma involving palms/soles, mucosal hyperemia, vomiting/diarrhea, myalgias with high CK, AKI and transaminitis, thrombocytopenia, negative blood cultures, and a retained tampon. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Toxic shock syndrome (menstrual, staphylococcal) (6). Cites the clinical criteria — fever, diffuse macular erythroderma, hypotension, and >=3 organ-system involvement with a likely toxin source (retained tampon) and negative blood cultures; explicitly excludes meningococcemia/other septic source, RMSF, and a severe drug reaction (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal, menstrual) — meets the clinical criteria: fever, diffuse macular erythroderma involving the palms/soles, hypotension, and multi-organ involvement (GI, muscular/high CK, renal AKI, hepatic, hematologic), with a likely toxin source (retained tampon) and negative blood cultures. Meningococcemia/other septic source is argued against by the absence of petechiae/purpura and meningismus, RMSF by the lack of travel/tick exposure and a non-petechial rash, and a severe drug reaction (DRESS/SJS-TEN) by no new medications and no bullae/Nikolsky sign.
mustNotMiss: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection or occult source

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH sepsis/TSS: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with vasopressors (e.g., norepinephrine) for refractory shock (CANT-MISS,6); SOURCE CONTROL — remove the tampon/debride/drain the focus (CANT-MISS,6); empiric antibiotics combining an antistaphylococcal/antistreptococcal agent (vancomycin) WITH a protein-synthesis inhibitor to suppress toxin (clindamycin or linezolid) (CANT-MISS,6); consider IVIG for severe/refractory disease (3); ICU admission and monitoring for multi-organ failure/DIC (2); blood cultures before antibiotics if it does not delay therapy (2). PENALTY: failing to obtain source control (-3); giving a beta-lactam alone without a toxin-suppressing agent (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: large-volume IV crystalloid and norepinephrine for fluid-refractory distributive shock; ICU admission. Achieve source control immediately — remove the retained tampon and debride/drain any infectious focus. Empiric antibiotics: vancomycin (cover MRSA/strep) PLUS clindamycin or linezolid to suppress toxin production (add a beta-lactam if streptococcal TSS). Consider IVIG in severe/refractory cases. Draw cultures before antibiotics if it does not delay care. Monitor and support for multi-organ failure and DIC. Counsel to avoid high-absorbency tampons.

criticalActions.item: Source control (remove tampon / debride or drain focus)
Empiric antibiotics including a toxin-suppressing agent (clindamycin/linezolid) plus vancomycin
Aggressive fluids and vasopressors for shock

coreActions.item: ICU admission with monitoring for multi-organ failure/DIC
Blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay

penalties.item: Beta-lactam alone without a toxin-suppressing agent

requiredActions: Source control (remove tampon / debride or drain focus)
Empiric antibiotics including a toxin-suppressing agent (clindamycin/linezolid) plus vancomycin
Aggressive fluids and vasopressors for shock
optionalActions: ICU admission with monitoring for multi-organ failure/DIC
Blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay
unsafeActions: Beta-lactam alone without a toxin-suppressing agent

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues; feels unwell and is a little dazed
standardizedPatient.affect: 23-year-old woman, ill-appearing, flushed, and lightheaded. Cooperative but feels awful and mildly dazed.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

historyTriggers.response: Yes, I'm on my period right now. I've been using the super-absorbent tampons and I think I left the last one in way too long — I forgot about it for almost a day.
[Reveal the relevant detail from patientFile only when asked directly; phrase in lay language.]
It came on really fast — the fever and feeling awful started last night, only about a day ago, and the rash showed up today.

triggerConcepts: what does the rash look like
historyTriggers.response: My whole body looks like a bad sunburn — it's red all over, even my palms. It hasn't blistered or peeled.
I've been throwing up and having watery diarrhea, my muscles ache all over, my head hurts, and my throat is sore. I keep feeling like I'll faint when I stand.
I only take the birth control pill. I haven't started any new medicines or antibiotics.
No travel, no hiking, no tick bites that I know of, and no one around me has been sick.
My head aches but my neck doesn't feel stiff and bright lights don't bother me especially.
I'm generally healthy with no medical problems, no recent surgery, and no wounds.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin: diffuse blanching macular erythroderma resembling a sunburn over trunk and extremities, including palms and soles; NO petechiae, NO purpura, NO vesicles/bullae, NO target lesions, NO Nikolsky sign; no desquamation yet.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: No rash; skin warm and dry.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin: NO petechiae and NO purpura (argues against meningococcemia/DIC-driven purpura).
HEENT: bilateral conjunctival injection (red eyes) without discharge.
HEENT: oropharyngeal hyperemia with a strawberry-appearing red tongue/throat; mucous membranes dry.
Neck supple with NO meningismus (helps argue against meningococcal meningitis).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 23F presents with about a day of high fever, a diffuse sunburn-like rash, vomiting/diarrhea, myalgias, and lightheadedness, now hypotensive. The diagnosis is Toxic shock syndrome, reached by eliciting the focused history (including menstrual/tampon use), performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Abrupt high fever with diffuse macular sunburn-like rash and hypotension
Vomiting, diarrhea, myalgias, and mucosal symptoms (multi-system)
Menstrual tampon use with a retained, prolonged-dwell tampon
No new medications, no travel/tick exposure (argues against drug reaction and tick-borne illness)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Skin: diffuse blanching macular erythroderma resembling a sunburn over trunk and extremities, including palms and soles; NO petechiae, NO purpura, NO vesicles/bullae, NO target lesions, NO Nikolsky sign; no desquamation yet.
Skin: NO petechiae and NO purpura (argues against meningococcemia/DIC-driven purpura).
HEENT: bilateral conjunctival injection (red eyes) without discharge.
HEENT: oropharyngeal hyperemia with a strawberry-appearing red tongue/throat; mucous membranes dry.
Neck supple with NO meningismus (helps argue against meningococcal meningitis).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (menstrual/tampon use, sexual history) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Toxic shock syndrome while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Toxic shock syndrome and manage with stabilization, source control, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Beta-lactam alone without a toxin-suppressing agent
managementPearls: Source control (remove tampon / debride or drain focus)
Empiric antibiotics including a toxin-suppressing agent (clindamycin/linezolid) plus vancomycin
Aggressive fluids and vasopressors for shock
criticalWorkupItems: Comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and hepatic function)
Cultures plus source identification and pelvic exam for retained tampon
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
No acute process (clear baseline; watch for evolving ARDS).
criticalManagementItems: Source control (remove tampon / debride or drain focus)
Empiric antibiotics including a toxin-suppressing agent (clindamycin/linezolid) plus vancomycin
Aggressive fluids and vasopressors for shock
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental or near-normal study is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
